---
title: "ai-minerals"
subtitle: "Mineral prospectivity mapping on public open data"
---

This site is a portfolio. Each project asks, on a different geological
problem, the same question: how far can a careful machine-learning
pipeline trained on public data go toward narrowing where to drill?
The work is aimed at AI-mineral-exploration teams (KoBold Metals,
ExploreTech, Earth AI) and is meant to demonstrate working knowledge
of the methodology and the places where the methodology breaks.

The methodology is straightforward. Federal and state agencies publish
several decades of mineral-occurrence records, geologic maps,
geophysical surveys, stream-sediment geochemistry, and remote-sensing
imagery. Resampled onto a common grid, each cell of land becomes a
feature vector. A Random Forest is trained to recognize the cells that
contain a known deposit, holding out spatial blocks so it cannot cheat
by memorizing neighbors. The output is a per-cell score, mapped back
across the region: where the model thinks the unknown deposits are.

What separates a portfolio model from an academic exercise is what
happens after the score map is drawn. The methodology has four
well-known traps (training-positive inflation; spatial leakage;
exploration bias in the labels; cross-region transfer failure). All
four show up in the work below.

# California Mother Lode (orogenic gold)

The current project. The Sierra Nevada foothills hold the historic
Mother Lode gold belt; the engineering challenge is assembling,
aligning, and feature-engineering a heterogeneous mix of public
datasets so a model can learn from them.

The pipeline pulls together six independent sources onto a shared
500-metre grid in California Albers projection:

- USGS aeromagnetic field plus four standard derivatives (first
  vertical derivative, horizontal gradient magnitude, analytic
  signal, tilt), each highlighting a different geometric property
  of the underlying rocks.
- USGS isostatic and Bouguer gravity, sensitive to crustal-density
  variations between the Sierra Nevada batholith and the metamorphic
  foothills belt that hosts the gold.
- USGS State Geologic Map Compilation lithology, with three levels
  of granularity (top-level GENERALIZED_LITH plus the MAJOR1, MAJOR2,
  and MAJOR3 free-text named lithologies, factorized to integer codes
  and one-hot encoded).
- USGS National Geochemical Database stream-sediment chemistry across
  the orogenic-Au pathfinder suite (Au, As, Sb, Hg, W, Ag plus base
  metals).
- Sentinel-2 SWIR-derived alteration indices (iron-oxide, ferrous,
  clay, NDVI), with cloud and vegetation masking applied per scene.
- USGS Mineral Resource Data System occurrence records, filtered to
  in-place orogenic gold via Cox-Singer-style screening of placer and
  alluvial records.

Each layer is fetched from public APIs, reprojected, gridded, and
joined into a single feature frame. The figure below shows the
magnetic-field derivatives over the Mother Lode AOI as one example
of the feature engineering involved.

![Magnetic-field derivatives over the Mother Lode AOI: first vertical derivative (sharpens shallow-source edges), horizontal gradient magnitude (peaks over rock contacts), analytic signal (orientation-independent edge detector), and tilt (equalizes anomaly amplitudes so weak features compete with strong ones).](data/derived/motherlode/fig_magnetic_derivatives_grid.png){width=100%}

Downstream of the pipeline, a Random Forest baseline trained on the
cleaned positive set plus spatial pseudo-negatives gives mean ROC-AUC
0.85 (95% CI [0.79, 0.90]) under 20-km spatial-block
cross-validation across 62 valid folds. The model is a validation of
the pipeline rather than a state-of-the-art predictor; deep-learning
prospectivity approaches like DEEP-SEAM and graph-based methods do
better in the published literature.

The interesting result that survived the validation tightening was a
methodology negative: cross-region transfer remains hard. A model
trained on the Sierra Mother Lode and applied to the Klamath
Mountains (a separate orogenic-Au province in northern California)
ranks Klamath cells at-or-below random at most cutoffs, even when the
regional-shifting features (geophysics, stream-sediment chemistry,
remote sensing) are stripped out. Geology-only transfer raises the
very top of the ranking to 1.27× random at top 1% but falls below
random for deeper cutoffs. The Sierra and Klamath belts share
lithology categories at the geologic-map level; the spatial context
of those lithologies differs enough that a model trained on one does
not generalize to the other. This is the unsolved frontier that
foundation-model approaches like GFM4MPM are trying to
address.[^gfm4mpm]

[^gfm4mpm]: Daruna et al., "GFM4MPM: Towards Geospatial Foundation
    Models for Mineral Prospectivity Mapping," 2024. The proposal
    is to pretrain a vision-style encoder on geophysical and
    geological grids so that downstream prospectivity classifiers
    inherit a transferable representation rather than learning
    region-specific spatial-context patterns from scratch.

# DEEP-SEAM independent replication

A separate diagnostic exercise: take the published DEEP-SEAM
mineral-prospectivity model (Luo et al. 2026, *Geosci. Model Dev.*),
re-implement the underlying Deviation Network architecture from the
paper alone (without consulting the authors' code), and check whether
the published headline result reproduces. Their dataset (Curnamona
REE, South Australia, 7 known deposits across 870 feature cells) is
publicly released on Zenodo.[^deepseam]

Result: under leave-one-out training over the seven positives, the
re-implemented model places every held-out deposit inside the top 5%
of the score distribution and inside the top 30% as the original
paper reports. Three of seven held-out positives land inside the top
1% of cells. The headline 86% of deposits in the top 2% number
underperforms slightly (we recover 43% at that cutoff), which is
consistent with hyperparameter or measurement-methodology gaps not
specified in the paper alone. The qualitative result reproduces.

The same dataset run through a Random Forest of equivalent
configuration is materially worse: only 43% of held-out positives
inside the top 5%, only 57% inside the top 30%. The DEEP-SEAM
architecture is doing real work over classical ensemble methods on
that data.

When the same Deviation Network is applied to the California Mother
Lode feature frame, the architecture does not transfer. The
anomaly-detection framing assumes positives are feature-space
outliers in a sea of "normal" unlabeled samples, which holds for
seven REE deposits in a tightly curated South Australian dataset. It
does not hold for 6,149 orogenic-gold positives spread densely across
the Mother Lode foothills belt. Out-of-distribution scoring on
unlabeled cells produces unbounded outputs that crowd out the true
positives at the top of the ranking. This is a structural
incompatibility between the model's framing and the data's shape, not
a preprocessing gap that more PCA decomposition fixes.

The takeaway from the 2×2 (their method × their data, our method ×
their data, their method × our data, our method × our data): the
methodology stack is sound and produces results consistent with the
data's structure on each problem. Cross-region generalization remains
the open frontier; deep-learning architectures alone do not solve it.

[^deepseam]: Luo et al., "DEEP-SEAM: an explainable semi-supervised
    deep learning framework for mineral prospectivity mapping,"
    *Geosci. Model Dev.* 19, 2593 (2026). Implementation in this
    portfolio at `src/ai_minerals/model_devnet.py`, ported from the
    Pang et al. 2019 deviation-networks paper that DEEP-SEAM itself
    builds on. The replication scripts are in
    `scripts/deep_seam_cell_{a,b,c}*.py`.

# Eastern Alaska and BC Golden Triangle (porphyry copper)

Two earlier projects covered porphyry copper-molybdenum systems in
Alaska's Yukon-Tanana terrane and British Columbia's Stikine arc.
Both used the same Random Forest plus spatial-block CV methodology
applied to two regions hosting world-class porphyry deposits
(Brucejack, KSM, Red Chris, Galore Creek).

The notable result was an external blind test on the Alaska model.
Kenorland's `23ETD062`, a 2023 porphyry intersection that postdates
every training label, scored at the 62nd percentile of the
prospectivity ranking. A diagnostic traced the failure to
*exploration bias*: areas with denser historical sampling had
denser positive labels, regardless of underlying geology. The
model had learned where geologists had previously looked, not
where the rocks favored mineralization. Four remediations (drop
sample-count features, mark missing data with indicators, train on
no-geochemistry features, switch to PU bagging) all failed to
rescue the blind test. The lesson is that an un-corrected
pseudo-supervised pipeline at regional resolution cannot
distinguish a distal low-grade porphyry hit from background. It
can rank the historic-sampling intensity, which is correlated but
not identical.

The BC Golden Triangle model was validated against a distribution
of 241 post-2015 drill holes (154 intersected positives, 87
drilled-negative) rather than a single intersection, which gives a
truer view of operating-point precision. The same exploration-bias
caveat applies in a milder form.

# Bear Cub (dark-data pilot)

A working pipeline that ingests 24 hand-written 1919-1955 paper
drill logs from a placer-gold property held by the user's family in
Nome, Alaska. The pipeline extracts hole locations and assay values
via OCR, cross-checks them against a 1955 cartographer's property
map, and derives the original-operator's empirical formula for
converting drill-log assay numbers into ounces per cubic yard. The
cross-check caught a 45° geomorphology error in the cartographer's
hole-numbering, which an OCR-confidence threshold alone would not
have surfaced.

The project demonstrates a methodology relevant to junior-mining
acquisition workflows: a meaningful fraction of historical mining
data exists only as scanned paper, and a self-correcting ingestion
pipeline (OCR plus a structural-consistency check against a known
external reference) can turn that data into modern feature vectors.
A 3D PyVista model of the drill-hole intersections is included.

# Themes

What the four projects together demonstrate:

- **Validation discipline matters more than the headline metric.**
  Spatial-block CV, bootstrap confidence intervals, external blind
  tests, and held-out cross-region transfer are the tools that
  separate a real result from an apparently-good one. Three of the
  four projects above show one of these checks falsifying a
  result that point-estimate metrics had endorsed.
- **Label cleanup beats feature engineering on this class of problem.**
  The largest single improvement on Mother Lode came from filtering
  20% of the positives that did not match the deposit type the model
  was trying to learn. New features mostly substituted for old ones.
- **Cross-region transfer is the unsolved frontier.** Sierra to
  Klamath fails. Eastern Alaska to a future Kenorland-style
  intersection fails. The published foundation-model approaches
  (GFM4MPM) are trying to build encoders that survive this kind of
  shift, and that is the direction the next round of work in this
  space is likely to come from.
- **Public data plus open libraries reach a working model.** None
  of the projects above used proprietary data feeds or hosted
  ML platforms. USGS, GSC, NGDB, SGMC, MRDS, and Sentinel-2 plus
  scikit-learn and Quarto are the entire stack.

# Reproduce

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd ai-minerals
uv sync
uv run python -m ai_minerals.data.fetch.all
uv run python -m ai_minerals.features.assemble
QUARTO_PYTHON=.venv/bin/python quarto render
```

License: MIT.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
import datetime as _dt
print(f"\n---\n\n*Site rendered {_dt.date.today():%Y-%m-%d}.*")
```
